prompt:
I own the self-healing policy layer of our YAML workflow DSL and I need it rebuilt as healbudget.go (package healbudget) in this repo. Other modules own expressions, branching and composition — this one executes linear set/call documents and decides WHAT HAPPENS WHEN A HANDLER FAILS: per-task healing metadata, a shared run-wide healing budget, an approval gate, and a deterministic attempt timeline with exponential backoff on an injected clock. There is deliberately NO expression engine here — the data-flow layer resolves values before documents reach this module, so every YAML value passes through verbatim (a string like "${ .x }" is just a string).

Housekeeping: the go.mod/go.sum pin gopkg.in/yaml.v3 v3.0.1 — use it for parsing, fetch deps with `go mod download`, and don't touch healbudget_test.go, go.mod, or go.sum. The engine must never read wall-clock time or really sleep: the ONLY clock is the injected one, and `go test -race` runs everything synchronously.

API (all in package healbudget):
- `Run(source string, opts Options) (*Result, error)` — the returned error is for load/config problems only; runtime failures come back inside Result.
- `Options{Handlers map[string]Handler, Approver Approver, Clock Clock}` with `type Handler func(args map[string]any) (any, error)`, `type Approver func(ApprovalRequest) bool`, `type Clock interface { Now() time.Duration; Sleep(time.Duration) }`. A nil Clock means an internal logical clock starting at zero with the same semantics (never time.Sleep).
- `type LoadError struct{ ... }` (works with errors.As) for document validation; `var ErrUnknownHandler` (works with errors.Is) — EVERY call task's handler name is checked against Options.Handlers up front, before ANY handler runs; the error names the missing handler.
- `type ApprovalRequest struct { Task string; Attempt int; Reason string }` — Attempt is the attempt number being requested (2 = first retry), Reason carries the last failure's error text.
- `type Attempt struct { Task string; Number int; At time.Duration; OK bool; Detail string }` — Number is 1-based per task, At is the clock reading when the attempt started, Detail is the error text for failed attempts and empty for successes.
- `type ErrorInfo struct { Task, Kind, Detail string }` with Kind one of "handler", "task-budget", "shared-budget", "approval-denied", "no-approver".
- `type Result struct { Status string; Context map[string]any; Timeline []Attempt; Healed int; Error *ErrorInfo }` — Status "completed" or "failed", Context maps task names to results, Timeline records every handler invocation in order, Healed counts shared-budget units consumed, Error is nil on completion.

Document shape + load validation (LoadError): `document:` with dsl exactly "1.0" plus non-empty namespace and name (message should name the missing field). `do:` non-empty ordered list of single-key maps, unique task names, each task exactly one of `set:` (a map, stored verbatim under the task name) or `call:` (handler name; optional `with:` map passed verbatim as args; the return value is stored under the task name). Tasks may carry `metadata:` with keys limited to description/expects/produces (strings) and `self_healing:`.

The policy blocks: per task, `metadata.self_healing` with keys limited to enabled (bool), retry_budget, approval_required (bool, default false) — anything else is a LoadError naming the stray key. When enabled is true: (a) retry_budget must be a positive integer (missing or non-positive is a LoadError mentioning retry_budget); (b) the metadata must be COMPLETE — description, expects, and produces all present as non-empty strings, and the LoadError names whichever is missing (a healing task someone can't reason about from its metadata is worse than no healing); (c) self-healing is only valid on call tasks — on a set task it's a LoadError naming the task; (d) the workflow must declare the shared pool: top-level `self_healing:` with `budget:` (positive integer; the LoadError for a missing/invalid one mentions budget) and optional `base_delay_seconds:` (positive integer, default 1); unknown keys there are LoadErrors too. A task with enabled false (or no self_healing block at all) needs none of this.

Runtime policy, exactly this order when a call task's handler returns an error: record the failed attempt in the timeline first, always. If the task doesn't have healing enabled, the run FAILS: Status "failed", Error {Task, Kind "handler", Detail = the handler's error text}, later tasks don't run (their names never appear in Context). Otherwise, to attempt retry k of that task (k counts the task's own retries, 1-based; Attempt.Number will be k+1):
1. Task budget: if k > retry_budget, fail with Kind "task-budget".
2. Shared budget: if the run has already consumed all `budget` units, fail with Kind "shared-budget". The pool is shared across ALL tasks in the run.
3. Approval gate: if approval_required, consult Options.Approver with {Task, Attempt: k+1, Reason: last error text} BEFORE backing off — a denial fails the run right there with Kind "approval-denied", no sleep, no budget consumed. approval_required with a nil Approver fails the same way with Kind "no-approver". The approver is consulted before EVERY retry of such a task, not just the first.
4. Backoff: Sleep(base_delay_seconds * 2^(k-1) seconds) on the injected clock — base, doubled each further retry of that task (2s, 4s, 8s... with base 2).
5. Re-invoke the handler, consuming one shared unit (Healed increments) and recording the attempt (At = clock reading at invocation). Success stores the result and the workflow moves on; another error loops back to step 1.

Done = `go test -race -timeout 30s ./...` green.
